S20: Publication status and date: New search for studies and content updated (no change to conclusions), published in Issue 6, 2013.
S21: Citation: Brettschneider J, Kurent J, Ludolph A.
S22: Drug therapy for pain in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or motor neuron disease.
S23: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 2013, Issue 6.
S29: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. A B S T R A C T Background Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is the most common neurodegenerative disorder of the motor system in adults.
S30: Pain in ALS is a frequent symptom especially in the later stages of disease and can have a pronounced influence on quality of life and suﬀering.
S31: Treatment of pain therefore should be recognised as an important aspect of palliative care in ALS.
S32: This is an update of a review first published in 2008.
S33: Objectives To systematically review the evidence for the eﬀicacy of drug therapy in relieving pain in ALS.
S34: We also aimed to evaluate possible adverse eﬀects associated with the diﬀerent drugs and their influence on survival and quality of life.
S35: Search methods On 2 July 2012, we searched the following databases: the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register (2 July 2012), CENTRAL (2012, Issue 6 in The Cochrane Library), MEDLINE (January 1966 to June 2012), EMBASE (January 1980 to June 2012), CINAHL (January 1982 to June 2012), AMED (January 1985 to June 2012) and LILACS (January 1982 to June 2012).
S36: We checked the bibliographies of trials identified and contacted other disease experts to identify further published and unpublished trials.
S37: Selection criteria We searched for randomised or quasi-randomised controlled trials on drug therapy for pain in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
S38: Data collection and analysis We collected data using a specially designed form and analysed them using the Cochrane Review Manager sofware.
S39: Main results We found no randomised or quasi-randomised controlled trials on drug therapy for pain in ALS or MND.
S40: Authors' conclusions There is no evidence from randomised controlled trials about the management of pain in ALS.
S41: Further research on this important aspect of palliative care in ALS is needed.
S42: Randomised controlled trials should be initiated to determine the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent analgesics for treatment of pain in ALS.
S43: Drug therapy for pain in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or motor neuron disease (Review) Copyright © 2013 The Cochrane Collaboration.

S47: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews P L A I N L A N G U A G E S U M M A R Y Drug therapy for pain in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or motor neuron disease Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is the most common neurodegenerative disorder of the motor system in adults.
S48: Pain is a frequent symptom and may have a pronounced impact on quality of life and suﬀering.
S49: Despite an extensive search of diﬀerent medical databases, this review was not able to identify any randomised controlled trials on drug therapy for pain in ALS.
S50: Currently (to July 2012) no evidence exists for using one type of treatment over another.
S51: Drug therapy for pain in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or motor neuron disease (Review) Copyright © 2013 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S52: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 2 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S55: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews B A C K G R O U N D Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is the most common neurodegenerative disorder of the motor system in adults.
S56: The incidence is up to 2 per 100,000 population per year and age specific incidence and mortality rates peak between 55 and 75 years (Worms 2001).
S57: People with ALS show a combination of upper and lower motor neuron signs and death usually results from respiratory failure.
S58: Over 50% of people with ALS die within three years of the first symptom.
S59: Although less of an issue in the earlier stages of the disease, pain has been reported to be a frequent symptom in the later stages of ALS (Ganzini 1999), where studies have reported pain to occur in up to 80% of cases (Gubbay 1985; Miller 1999; Newrick 1985; Saunders 1981; Wu 1998).
S60: Pain mainly aﬀects the lower back, shoulders, neck and legs (Ganzini 1999; Wu 1998) and may have a dull or electrical character (Newrick 1984; Newrick 1985).
S61: Patients sometimes report pain of a diﬀerent quality in diﬀerent parts of the body.
S62: Although in most cases the severity of the pain is reported to be mild or moderate, severe pain is reported in 20% of patients (Ganzini 1999; Newrick 1985).
S63: It has been suggested that the intensity of pain increases with disease duration (Drory 2001).
S64: The aetiology of pain in patients with ALS is not well understood.
S65: Musculoskeletal pain may arise in the later stages of disease as a result of stress on bones and joints that have lost their protective muscular sheath due to atrophy (Borasio 1997; Borasio 2001a).
S66: In addition, pain may be caused by muscle contractures and joint stiﬀness (Borasio 1997; Borasio 2001a).
S67: This may explain why pain seems to be a particular feature in the later stages of the disease.
S68: Pain may also arise from muscle cramps and muscle spasticity.
S69: Another cause of pain in patients with ALS seems to be skin pressure caused by immobility (Borasio 1997).
S70: In this context, decubitus ulcers have been reported in 16% of patients (Saunders 1981).
S71: Pain may also occur as a manifestation of the sensory disturbances which are reported to occur in up to 11% of patients (Lawyer 1953).
S72: Pathological involvement of sensory pathways in motor neuron disease has only infrequently been reported (Brownell 1970).
S73: A potentially associated chronic central pain syndrome was reported in a single patient (Drake 1983).
S74: The first line treatment for pain in ALS is usually non- opioid analgesics, including acetaminophen, or non-steroidal anti- inflammatory agents (NSAIDs) (Ganzini 1999; Wu 1998).
S75: Opioid drugs are usually used if these drugs fail to relieve the pain (O'Brien 1992).
S76: Opioids may also be used for the symptomatic treatment of dyspnoea and coughing in MND (Neudert 2001; Oliver 1998).
S77: There is considerable discrepancy in the literature regarding the frequency of dosing and actual dose provided, as well as the routes of administration of opioids in ALS (Ganzini 1999; Neudert 2001; Wu 1998).
S78: Opioids are possibly more frequently used in patients treated in hospices or palliative care units (Neudert 2001).
S79: It is possible that some health professionals might be hesitant to use opioids for fear that they might hasten death by suppressing ventilation (Oliver 1998) and because patients and family caregivers might associate opiate drugs with terminal stages of disease and imminent death (Oliver 1998).
S80: Continuing uncertainty over the indications, doses, application modes and side eﬀects of pain medication in the palliative therapy of patients with ALS suggests that a systematic review of all randomised clinical trials concerning treatment of pain in ALS is required.
S81: The results will need to be considered in the context of non-randomised studies in the Discussion section of this review.
S82: O B J E C T I V E S The objective of this review was to systematically review the evidence for the eﬀicacy of drug therapy in relieving pain in ALS.
S83: We also aimed to evaluate possible adverse eﬀects associated with the diﬀerent drugs and their influence on survival and quality of life.
S84: M E T H O D S Criteria for considering studies for this review Types of studies All randomised and quasi-randomised (alternate or other systematic allocation) controlled studies involving drug therapy administered to relieve pain in ALS or MND.
S85: We did not include studies of treatments for cramps in ALS or MND as this is the topic of another Cochrane review (Baldinger 2012).
S86: Types of participants All those with a clinical diagnosis of ALS or MND (pure mixed upper motor neuron and lower motor neuron degeneration with a supportive electromyogram) according to the revised El Escorial criteria (Brooks 2000)) and with any clinical pattern of the condition (e.g.
S87: bulbar or limb onset) who report experiencing pain.
S88: Types of interventions Any drug therapy, given by any route, in any dose, administered to relieve pain in ALS/MND.
S89: Types of outcome measures Primary outcomes The primary outcome was patient reported pain relief afer 24 hours (site, intensity, frequency and duration of pain) measured by a visual analogue scale, verbal or numerical rating scale, or any other validated assessment tool.
S90: Secondary outcomes The secondary outcome measures were: (1) patient reported pain relief afer seven days; (2) adverse eﬀects of treatment.
S91: An assessment of the severity and frequency of the reported adverse eﬀects was made.
S92: Adverse eﬀects were categorised into those severe enough to result in cessation of treatment (for example respiratory depression as an adverse eﬀect of opioids) and those that were mild.
S93: Wherever possible, we planned to transform visual analogue scales and any categorical outcomes and analyse them as dichotomous data.
S94: Search methods for identification of studies (1) We searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register (2 July 2012), CENTRAL (2012, Issue 6 in The Cochrane Library), MEDLINE (January 1966 to June 2012), EMBASE (January 1980 to June 2012), CINAHL (January 1982 to June 2012), AMED (January 1985 to June 2012) and LILACS (January 1982 to June 2012).
S95: Drug therapy for pain in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or motor neuron disease (Review) Copyright © 2013 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S96: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 3 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S99: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews (2) In addition, we reviewed the results of the RCT searches of MEDLINE (January 1966 to June 2012) and EMBASE (January 1980 to June 2012) for well-designed published observational studies of pain therapy in terminal ALS, with controls, where the diagnosis was clearly stated, the interventions were clearly described and the outcomes were clearly reported for all patients.
S100: For example, we collected prospective cohort studies or case control studies with concurrent controls.
S101: We also considered retrospective case series of more than five participants where patients were treated consecutively.
S102: This strategy was deemed necessary because we suspected there would be few or no randomised controlled trials or quasi-randomised controlled trials on this particular topic, as we believed it would be unethical to design a placebo controlled trial for treatment of pain in ALS.
S103: For this update, on 9 April 2013, we also searched the US national Institutes of Health registry ClinicalTrials.gov (http:// clinicaltrials.gov) and the World Health Organization (WHO) International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (ICTRP) (http:// apps.who.int/trialsearch/) for ongoing trials.
S104: Electronic searches The detailed search strategies are in the appendices:MEDLINE (Appendix 1), EMBASE (Appendix 2), CINAHL Plus (Appendix 3), AMED (Appendix 4), LILACS (Appendix 5), CENTRAL Appendix 6, ClinicalTrials.gov and WHO ICTRP (Appendix 7).
S105: Searching other resources We planned to check the bibliographies of trials identified and contacted other disease experts for further published and unpublished trials.
S106: Data collection and analysis Two authors (previously JB, JD Mitchell (JDM); for this update, JB and JK) checked all titles and abstracts identified by the searches for randomised or quasi-randomised trials.
S107: Two authors (previously JB, JDM; for this update, JB and JK) also checked all titles and abstracts identified by the searches for non-randomised evidence.
S108: The authors obtained the full texts of all potentially relevant studies and assessed them independently.
S109: Two authors extracted data independently onto a specially designed form (JB, JDM; not required for this update).
S110: One author (JB) entered the data into the sofware and two checked the data entered (JK, AL).
S111: All the authors decided which trials met the inclusion criteria for the review.
S112: Disagreement about inclusion was resolved by discussion and consensus between the authors.
S113: Assessment of risk of bias in included studies Had there been RCTs two authors would have independently assessed risk of bias using in the following domains using the criteria described in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins 2011): • secure randomisation; • allocation concealment; • blinding of subject and observer; • blinding of outcome assessors; • completeness of follow up and intention-to-treat analysis; • selective outcome reporting; • other sources of bias.
S114: We would have quantified the risk of bias as follows: Low risk of bias, High risk of bias, or Unclear risk of bias (insuﬀicient detail reported, risk of bias unknown or the entry is not relevant to the study).
S115: Where there was an unclear risk of bias , we would have tried to obtain further information from the trial investigators.
S116: Data collection We designed a data collection form for the review and planned to collect the following data: • publication details; • participants: number of patients, gender, mean age, disease duration, clinical subtype of ALS (bulbar, limb, both bulbar and limb); • trial quality characteristics; • site of pain; • cause of pain; • duration of pain; • description of intervention; • outcome data; • adverse eﬀects.
S117: We planned to obtain missing or additional data from the authors wherever possible.
S118: Data analysis The Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group guidelines were followed.
S119: We planned to analyse the data using Cochrane Review Manager sofware (currently RevMan 2012) and to report it according to the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (currently Higgins 2011).. We aimed to calculate the risk ratio (RR) and corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CI) for dichotomous data and the mean diﬀerence (MD) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) for continuous data.
S120: Analyses of subgroups identifying diﬀerent drug treatments and diﬀerent doses were to be carried out if suﬀicient data had been available.
S121: Wherever possible, we intended to compare diﬀerent subgroups of pain medication.
S122: We planned to test for heterogeneity across trials and if heterogeneity could be found we intended to undertake sensitivity analyses by repeating the calculation omitting trials that had low scores on individual quality items.
S123: If heterogeneity could not be explained by variation in trial quality we intended to use a random- eﬀects approach to obtain the pooled estimates from the group of trials.
S124: This review has a published protocol (Brettschneider 2005).
S125: If at a future update new trials emerge we will update the methodology according to Cochrane Collaboration guidance at that time.
S126: R E S U L T S Description of studies The number of references retrieved by the new, current electronic searches for the review were: MEDLINE 151 (10 new papers), EMBASE = 229 (76 new papers), AMED = 8 (1 new paper), LILACS = 0, CINAHL Plus = 27 (6 new papers), CENTRAL = 13 and the Drug therapy for pain in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or motor neuron disease (Review) Copyright © 2013 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S127: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 4 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S130: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Cochrane NMD Group Specialised Register 15 (1 new paper).
S131: Afer screening, no randomised or quasi-randomised controlled trials on drug therapy for pain in ALS or MND were found.
S132: There was one randomised controlled study on gabapentin (which is ofen used for treatment of pain) in ALS, but pain was not an outcome measure in that study (Miller 2001).
S133: We could find no well designed published observational studies on pain therapy in ALS.
S134: We found one Cochrane review on treatment for spasticity in ALS/MND, although it identified no randomised trials examining the eﬀectiveness of anti-spasticity drugs (Ashworth 2006).
S135: In total there are 34 excluded studies.
S136: At this update we excluded Weber 2010, which is included in the Cochrane review of treatments for cramp in ALS (Baldinger 2012).
S137: We removed three reviews from the excluded studies tables (Ashworth 2006; Borasio 1997; Borasio 2001b).
S138: We found 13 case series of more than five participants describing drug therapy for pain in ALS (Amtmann 2004; Bedlack 2009; Ganzini 1999; Ganzini 2002b; McClelland 2008; Neudert 2001; Newrick 1984; Newrick 1985; O'Brien 1992; Oliver 1996; Oliver 1998; Saunders 1981; Wu 1998).
S139: Further data included case series (Adelman 2004; Abe 2008; Bradley 2001; de Castro-Costa 1999; Ganzini 1998; Hecht 2002; Jensen 2005; Tedman 1997; Trail 2003; Winter 2010) and case reports (de Carvalho 2001; Drake 1983; Shibata 2003) as well as surveys of expert opinion (Abe 2008; Borasio 2001a; Carter 2010; Chio 2001; Forshew 2003) or caregivers' experience (Ganzini 2002a; Maessen 2010; Mandler 2001) describing certain aspects such as causes, frequency, localisation or intensity of pain in ALS.
S140: We found no relevant ongoing studies.
S141: Risk of bias in included studies No evidence above the level of case series was found.
S142: Eﬀects of interventions We did not find any randomised or quasi-randomised controlled trials on drug therapy for pain in ALS or MND in the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialised Register, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL), MEDLINE, EMBASE, CINAHL, LILACS or AMED, or any other sources.
S143: D I S C U S S I O N Frequency of pain Evidence derived from case series, surveys of expert opinion and case reports showed pain in ALS to occur in between 3% and 78% of patients (Bradley 2001; Ganzini 1999; Maessen 2010; Winter 2010; Wu 1998).
S144: Frequency of pain has been shown to increase with ongoing disease (Hecht 2002).
S145: Intensity and relevance of pain Intensity of pain was shown to be mostly moderate in the earlier stages of ALS, though it was demonstrated to increase with ongoing disease (Adelman 2004; Ganzini 2002b).
S146: In a case series including 126 patients with MND, mean severity of pain was rated with 25.6 points on a 100 point visual analogue scale (20.8 to 30.4, 95% CI) (Clarke 2005).
S147: In the final stages of disease, pain was noted to be of severe intensity in up to 20% of patients (Ganzini 1998; Ganzini 1999; Ganzini 2002a; Ganzini 2002b).
S148: Agreement between patients and caregivers for ratings of pain level was shown to be high (Adelman 2004).
S149: Pain in ALS was shown to correlate significantly with depression ratings (Tedman 1997).
S150: Depression was shown to be associated a significantly reduced quality of life in ALS (Lou 2003).
S151: Furthermore, suﬀering was statistically correlated with increasing pain, and pain was the most significant contributor to suﬀering in the final stages of disease (Ganzini 1999).
S152: There was a marked interference of pain with activities of daily living in ALS patients (Jensen 2005).
S153: In the final stages of disease, pain was associated with a patient's interest in assisted suicide (Ganzini 2002b).
S154: Causes and localisation of pain Most frequently, pain was found to be associated with reduced joint mobility, cramps or skin pressure caused by immobility (Borasio 1997; Borasio 2001b; de Castro-Costa 1999; Ganzini 1999; Neudert 2001; Newrick 1984).
S155: There were also case reports of a chronic central pain syndrome in ALS (Drake 1983) as well as a case of complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) in an ALS patient without preceding trauma (Shibata 2003).
S156: Pain was most frequently localised in the lower back area, the legs, and in the shoulder and neck area of the patients (Ganzini 1999; Newrick 1985; Saunders 1981).
S157: Therapy for pain Acetaminophen or other NSAIDs were usually applied as first line treatment for pain in ALS (Saunders 1981; Wu 1998).
S158: Pain was ofen shown to be well controlled by these drugs in the earlier stages of disease (Saunders 1981).
S159: However, there was evidence that in the later stages of disease pain may not be controlled with these analgesics (Newrick 1984).
S160: Although pain was shown to be common, especially in the later stages of disease, it was found to be frequently undertreated (Ganzini 1999; Mandler 2001).
S161: In a study by Ganzini and colleagues, only 77% of patients with pain received a specific treatment (Ganzini 1999).
S162: These patients were treated with opioids in 40%, NSAIDs in 44% and acetaminophen in 19% (Ganzini 1999).
S163: Opioids were found to be an eﬀective therapy for pain, which also showed beneficial eﬀects on dyspnoea and insomnia (O'Brien 1992) (mean dose 30 (+/- 2.34) mg/24 hour oral morphine equivalent, mean duration of treatment 58 (+/- 18.51) days).
S164: Opioids were significantly more likely to be administered if patients were treated in a hospice or palliative care unit compared to those treated at home (Neudert 2001; Oliver 1996; Saunders 1981).
S165: There were distinct inter-country diﬀerences regarding the use of opioids: Neudert and colleagues observed morphine to be given to only 27% of a German ALS cohort as compared to 82% of a comparable UK cohort (Neudert 2001).
S166: A study in which oral morphine was used over an average of 95 days suggested that strong opioids could be used safely in palliative care and do not necessarily shorten life (Oliver 1998).
S167: One study reported that cannabis was eﬀective at reducing pain in ALS (Amtmann 2004).
S168: However, only about 10% of ALS patients surveyed in this study reported using cannabis, distinctly fewer than observed in other severe diseases, such as multiple sclerosis, AIDS or cancer patients (Amtmann 2004).
S169: Drug therapy for pain in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis or motor neuron disease (Review) Copyright © 2013 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S170: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 5 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S173: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Quinine may be an option for the treatment of muscle cramps in ALS in many countries, but is no longer approved for therapy of cramps in the United States as determined by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA 2006; FDA 2010).
S174: A survey of members of the European ALS Research Group showed that for treatment of cramps, quinine sulphate was used in 58% of centres, benzodiazepines in 40%, magnesium in 25% and carbamazepine in 23% (Borasio 2001a).
S175: Separate Cochrane reviews on quinine for the treatment of muscle cramps (El Tawil 2010) and treatment for muscle cramps in ALS (Baldinger 2012) have been published.
S176: Gabapentin (Miller 2001) and tetrahydrocannabinol (Weber 2010) were observed to have no significant eﬀect on muscle cramps.
S177: In contrast, a small open-label, non-controlled study suggested that levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily may be eﬀective in reducing the severity and frequency of cramps in ALS (Bedlack 2009).
S178: So far, there have been no randomised trials examining the eﬀectiveness of the existing anti-spasticity drugs in ALS (Ashworth 2006).
S179: According to a survey of expert opinion, as a therapy for spasticity, baclofen was used in 100% of large centres, tizanidine in 36%, benzodiazepines in 21% and dantrolene in 29% (Chio 2001).
S180: A survey of members of the European ALS Research Group showed that for the treatment of spasticity, carbamazepine was used in 93%, tizanidine in 38%, dantrolene in 36% and benzodiazepines in 36% (Borasio 2001a).
S181: The eﬀectiveness of these drugs on spasticity- associated pain was not monitored in either study.
S182: In a case series of eight ALS patients with intractable pain related to spasticity, intrathecal baclofen was able to achieve a pain score reduction in six patients, indicating that intrathecal application of baclofen could be an option to treat spasticity-related pain in patients that do not respond to oral therapy (McClelland 2008).
S183: Though there is some evidence from animal models that riluzole could be beneficial in neuropathic pain (Coderre 2007; Sung 2003), this has not been demonstrated in humans so far (Pittenger 2008).
S184: Pain as an underrated symptom in ALS An important goal of treatment in patients with ALS is to relieve symptoms, diminish suﬀering and improve quality of life.
S185: The studies we reviewed showed pain in ALS to be common, especially in the later stages of disease and to have a pronounced influence on quality of life and suﬀering (Ganzini 1999; Ganzini 2002b; Tedman 1997).
S186: Treatment for pain, therefore, should be recognised as an important aspect of palliative care in ALS.
S187: In contrast to its clinical relevance, data regarding the treatment for pain in ALS were found to be extremely scarce.
S188: As mentioned above, we found no randomised or quasi-randomised controlled trials or well-designed observational studies.
S189: This under-representation in the scientific literature in our opinion demonstrates that pain is still an underestimated symptom in ALS and may consequently be frequently undertreated.
S190: We found data regarding pain in ALS not only to be scarce, but also frequently inconsistent when describing certain characteristics.
S191: For example, we found pronounced diﬀerences between studies describing the prevalence or severity of pain.
S192: These discrepancies may be partially due to diﬀerences among the cohorts included in the studies, for example, with regard to duration of disease.
S193: Data regarding the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent analgesics for therapy of pain in ALS were even rarer than data on the characteristics of pain in ALS or MND.
S194: The few available low evidence level studies seem to indicate that NSAIDs and acetaminophen, or both, may be eﬀective for moderate pain frequently observed in the earlier stages of disease (Saunders 1981; Wu 1998).
S195: However, if these therapies prove to be insuﬀicient, opioids seem to be an eﬀective and well- tolerated alternative that could also alleviate other symptoms such as dyspnoea and anxiety (Neudert 2001; O'Brien 1992; Oliver 1998).
S196: Treatment for pain in ALS is a common unresolved problem.
S197: In the absence of any evidence from randomised controlled trials, it is the authors' opinion that treatment for pain in ALS should follow the 1990 World Health Organization (WHO) Analgesic Ladder (WHO 1990).
S198: The WHO Analgesic Ladder includes a structured approach to the management of pain according to pain severity and the corresponding need for utilisation of drugs with the increasing potency required for eﬀective pain control.
S199: The Analgesic Ladder includes the major categories of analgesic agents that this review has identified as being in use for patients with ALS or MND.
S200: The recommendation for using the WHO Analgesic Ladder is also consistent with the overall symptom management goals discussed in the evidence-based practice parameter for care of the patient with ALS developed by the American Academy of Neurology (Miller 1999).
S201: A U T H O R S ' C O N C L U S I O N S Implications for practice There is no evidence from randomised controlled trials about the management of pain in ALS.
S202: Implications for research Further research on this important aspect of palliative care in ALS is needed.
S203: Randomised controlled trials should be initiated to determine the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent analgesics for the treatment of pain in ALS.
S204: Treatment of pain should be included as an outcome measure in clinical trials using drugs with a known analgesic capacity.
